bibliographic.title: Vindicta Salvatoris. Mittelenglisches Gedicht des 13. Jahrhunderts, zum erstenmal herausgegeben

entities.description: The independently published first installment, containing Fischer's title, description of Pepys MS 2014, source analysis, editorial method, production credits, and poem lines 1-1030. The XML begins only at the poem on p. 289 and omits the introduction on pp. 285-288.
entities.label: Rudolf Fischer, Vindicta Salvatoris, first installment (1903)
notes: The subtitle's thirteenth-century claim is an edition-layer historical assertion, not current witness dating.
Printed p. 285 follows the preceding article on a new page; p. 298 ends with Fortsetzung folgt.

rights.basis: Publication in 1903, U-M's public-domain source-edition policy, and Internet Archive's Not In Copyright/no-visible-notice record support US public-domain status.
rights.component: 1903 article text, transcription, commentary, and typography
notes: Copyright status outside the United States was not separately assessed.

bibliographic.citation: Rudolf Fischer, 'Vindicta Salvatoris. Mittelenglisches Gedicht des 13. Jahrhunderts, zum erstenmal herausgegeben, II. (Schluss),' Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 112 (1904), 25-45.

entities.description: The independently published concluding installment, continuing the poem with lines 1031-3114 on pp. 25-45 and adding Fischer's two-paragraph German closing commentary at the bottom of p. 45.
entities.label: Rudolf Fischer, Vindicta Salvatoris, second installment (1904)

notes: Printed p. 25 begins immediately after the preceding article; p. 45 contains line 3114 and the complete closing commentary before the following article begins on p. 46.

rights.basis: Publication in 1904, U-M's public-domain source-edition policy, and Internet Archive's Not In Copyright/no-visible-notice record support US public-domain status.
rights.component: 1904 article text, transcription, commentary, and typography
notes: Copyright status outside the United States was not separately assessed.

entities.description: The working manuscript transcript made at Cambridge for Fischer's edition. It is classified provisionally as a manuscript_witness because the schema has no dedicated working-transcript type; it is a modern intermediary, not a medieval textual witness.
entities.label: A. Reinke's working transcript of Pepys MS 2014
notes: Do not flatten the production route into a direct Fischer-to-codex transcription without acknowledging Reinke's intermediary copy.
Fischer does not state its support, extent, present repository, or whether Kiehl's collation was entered directly on it.

access.accessMethod: Read the exact specialist manuscript component description and linked work record.
notes: This exact component route is scholarly rather than an online holding-institution catalog; Magdalene's current guide states that no Pepys Library online catalog exists.
access.provider: University of York, Database of Middle English Romance

access.accessMethod: The current official Pepys Digital Library's enumerated digitized-manuscript selection was checked for shelfmark 2014.
notes: The page enumerated Pepys 878, 911, 1047, 1236, 1307, 1661, and a selected portion of 2981; it did not list Pepys 2014.
This is a dated negative for that named public selection, not proof that no internal, earlier, or future photographs exist. No manuscript images were cached.

access.accessMethod: Contact the Pepys Librarian in advance with credentials and the exact shelfmark. The Library reports limited research service under alternative arrangements during restoration.

access.accessMethod: Email the Pepys Librarian to request photographic services and separately ask about publication permission, formats, completeness, cost, and reuse terms.
notes: Request the entire publicly obtainable physical object rather than only the work: front and back binding boards/covers, spine, exposed edges when offered, turn-ins and pastedowns, all flyleaves/endleaves and blanks, and every manuscript leaf including adjacent Chronicle of Popes and Emperors and Robert of Gloucester material. Identify target ff. 23r-35v separately for work mapping.
Ask for a repository-confirmed image inventory and foliation crosswalk before describing a delivered set as complete.
Purchase or private-study permission does not authorize Git redistribution, publication, or for-profit print-on-demand use; the College separately requires publication permission.

rights.basis: Magdalene's official Pepys Digital Library states that its images are copyrighted, publication images must be purchased, and permission must be obtained before publication; no item-specific Pepys 2014 image license was available because no public object was found.
rights.component: any repository-supplied photographs or facsimile of Pepys MS 2014
notes: A private research copy, if supplied, must remain gitignored unless the College grants redistribution permission in writing.
Publication, repository redistribution, and for-profit or nonprofit print-on-demand use require separate item-specific confirmation; delivery alone is not authorization.

dateStatements.label: current specialist date
dateStatements.value: end of the 14th or start of the 15th century

entities.description: The parchment miscellany that supplied Fischer's only continuous base text. The short-version Titus and Vespasian occupies ff. 23r-35v between the Chronicle of Popes and Emperors and Robert of Gloucester's Metrical Chronicle.

holding.notes: The College's current catalog guide says there is no online Pepys Library catalog; the exact public component description used here is a University of York scholarly database record, not a holding-institution catalog URL.

entities.label: Cambridge, Magdalene College, Pepys MS 2014

notes: Current specialist description localizes production to Northamptonshire.
Contents recorded by the Database of Middle English Romance: Chronicle of Popes and Emperors, ff. 1r-20v; target poem, ff. 23r-35v; Robert of Gloucester's Metrical Chronicle, ff. 36r-62v.
The target is imperfect in 3,114 lines. The database says the opening 814 lines are lacking; Fischer says the first 812 verses are absent.

Fischer reports two ruled blank leaves after the first chronicle and later seventeenth-century headings over the target; full current codicology awaits the printed Pepys catalog or repository confirmation.

rights.basis: The anonymous medieval work and its 1903-1904 publication are far outside US copyright duration; this finding does not cover new photographs, catalog prose, or provider contracts.
rights.component: anonymous medieval Middle English text embodied in ff. 23r-35v

rights.basis: Magdalene's official Pepys Digital Library states that its images are copyrighted, publication images must be purchased, and permission must be obtained before publication; no item-specific Pepys 2014 image license was available because no public object was found.
rights.component: any repository-supplied photographs or facsimile of Pepys MS 2014
notes: A private research copy, if supplied, must remain gitignored unless the College grants redistribution permission in writing.
Publication, repository redistribution, and for-profit or nonprofit print-on-demand use require separate item-specific confirmation; delivery alone is not authorization.

entities.description: A later critical and contextual edition of the long version, used here for current title, genre, source, version, manuscript-census, and antisemitism evidence. It is not an ancestor of Fischer's edition or CME00072 and does not reproduce the Pepys short-version text as its base.

notes: The live Database of Middle English Romance page lists this modern edition as 2022. The official METS page and inspected title page say 2021, with digital publication in 2024, so those primary bibliographic dates are preferred while the catalog discrepancy is retained.

bibliographic.title: Review of Fritz Bergau, Untersuchungen über Quelle und Verfasser des mittelenglischen Reimgedichts: The vengeaunce of goddes deth

entities.description: The actual Archiv review of Fritz Bergau's 1901 dissertation, published in volume 108, pp. 199-206. It analyzes multiple sources of the wider Vengeaunce poem and distinguishes the alliterative Siege. Fischer's 1904 commentary names Suchier but prints volume/page coordinates that instead point back to Fischer's own first installment.
entities.label: Walther Suchier's review of Bergau's Vengeaunce study (1902)

rights.basis: The review was published in 1902 in the complete Archiv volume exposed by Internet Archive.

next_steps: Email pepyslibrary@magd.cam.ac.uk requesting the full physical object—front/back binding boards or covers, spine, exposed edges when offered, turn-ins and pastedowns, all flyleaves/endleaves and blanks, and every manuscript leaf—with target ff. 23r-35v mapped separately.

Ask for a repository-confirmed image count, foliation/inventory, resolution/color profile, delivery format, cost, private-storage permission, Git redistribution terms, publication permission, and commercial/noncommercial print-on-demand terms before any use beyond private research.
openQuestions.question: Can Magdalene College supply a complete practical-resolution image set for Pepys MS 2014, and under what private-use, redistribution, publication, and commercial terms?

next_steps: Email library@uu.nl for the M121 reel inventory, first and last manuscript images, image count, generation, target ff. 23r-35v mapping, consultation options, cost, and any digital-delivery route.
Request written terms separately for private storage, public redistribution, publication, and commercial or noncommercial print-on-demand; do not infer them from consultation availability.
openQuestions.question: Does Utrecht positive microfilm M121 reproduce the complete physical Pepys MS 2014, and can Utrecht supply or permit a digital surrogate under terms compatible with private research, public Git reference, publication, or print-on-demand use?

next_steps: Consult McKitterick and Beadle, Catalogue of the Pepys Library, volume V.i, or ask the Pepys Librarian for the MS 2014 entry and any later corrections.
Do not promote Fischer's 1903 paleography or ownership description into current catalog facts without confirmation.
openQuestions.question: What is the current holding-institution codicological description and full physical extent of Pepys MS 2014?

next_steps: Search Fischer, Brandl, Reinke, and Kiehl papers and ask the Pepys Library whether production correspondence or copies accompanied the edition.
Retain the transcript and collation as distinct evidenced production steps until the physical records are located.
openQuestions.question: Do A. Reinke's working transcript and B. Kiehl's collation record survive, and were Kiehl's corrections entered directly on the transcript or kept separately?

next_steps: Align Pepys line 1 against the current long-version edition and review Phyllis Moe's short/long version study; preserve both published counts until their lineation systems are reconciled.
openQuestions.question: How should Fischer's statement that 812 opening verses are absent be reconciled with the current specialist record's 814-line figure?

next_steps: Check correspondence, later notes, Bergau's complete work, and other Suchier publications; cite Archiv 108, pp. 199-206 only for the source study actually present there.
openQuestions.question: Where did Walther Suchier identify the Pepys text as a partial version of the wider Vengeaunce, given that Fischer's printed review coordinates are wrong and the actual 1902 review does not mention Pepys 2014?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Content and Collections for item-level keying, proofreading, source-copy, and selection notes; retain the directly observed page alignment meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: What item-level U-M production record explains the choice to omit Fischer's pp. 285-288 introduction while retaining the p. 45 German commentary?

relations.subjectLabel: Internet Archive complete provider scan of Archiv volume 108 (1902)

reviewNotes: Fischer's source-edition title Vindicta Salvatoris is retained at the edition layer; the current work title is a later authority decision.

The poem contains sustained Christian anti-Jewish polemic, collective blame, supersessionism, graphic violence, torture, and dehumanization. Descriptive language records those features without endorsing them.
Pepys 2014's manuscript production date and Northamptonshire origin are witness facts, not composition facts for the wider romance or short recension.

lineage.summary: CME00072 is the repository XML for a University of Michigan manually keyed and proofread electronic text derived from two independently published installments of Rudolf Fischer's edition. The XML omits Fischer's introductory pp. 285-288, encodes the Middle English poem on Archiv 111 (1903), pp. 289-298 and Archiv 112 (1904), pp. 25-45, and retains Fischer's two-paragraph German closing commentary on p. 45. Fischer worked through a Cambridge transcript made by A. Reinke and subsequently collated by B. Kiehl against Cambridge, Magdalene College, Pepys MS 2014, formerly Pepys 37; the poem occupies ff. 23r-35v. Current scholarship identifies this as an imperfect short-version witness of The Destruction of Jerusalem or Titus and Vespasian, not the separate alliterative Siege of Jerusalem and not a one-to-one copy of the Latin or Old English Vindicta Salvatoris. Complete public provider PDFs, OCR, metadata, and scan-data sidecars for Archiv volumes 111 and 112 are cached with the exact encoded runs mapped inside each volume. A complete modern METS comparison-edition PDF and Archiv 108 containing Walther Suchier's actual 1902 review are also cached as downstream supporting sources. Magdalene's named public Pepys Digital Library selection did not include Pepys 2014 when checked; whole-object photography and permission routes are recorded with the target folios kept separate. Utrecht University Library also lists a requestable positive microfilm, M121, but the public inventory does not establish the reel's extent or reproduction terms.

catalogingSubject.label: The Destruction of Jerusalem (short version, Pepys 2014)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous short version of the Middle English rhyming-couplet romance now usually called The Destruction of Jerusalem or Titus and Vespasian, as transmitted imperfectly in Cambridge, Magdalene College, Pepys MS 2014 and represented by Rudolf Fischer's two-part 1903-1904 edition and University of Michigan CME item CME00072. The record includes Fischer's brief German closing commentary because the XML includes it, but distinguishes that editorial prose from the medieval poem. It does not identify this work with the separate alliterative Siege of Jerusalem, with Latin or Old English texts titled Vindicta Salvatoris, with the long version of this romance, with the complete Pepys miscellany, or with the digital and repository artifacts that represent it.

contentParts.label: The Destruction of Jerusalem, short-version text from Pepys MS 2014
notes: The 27 XML verse groups reflect editorial grouping and initials, not a claim for 27 authorial chapters or stanzas.
The first and final textual losses or omissions are not quantified beyond the competing source statements.
contentParts.summary: The imperfect 3,114-line Middle English couplet text printed by Fischer across two journal installments.

contentParts.label: Fischer's closing commentary
contentParts.summary: Two German paragraphs declining full linguistic and stylistic analysis, identifying the printed text as a partial version of the wider Vengeaunce/Battle poem, reporting then-known witness counts, and referring to Bergau and Suchier.

assertion.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance dates the work, while modern scholarship distinguishes long and short versions. The interval is not a direct date for the act that formed the short recension.

dateStatements.display: 1375-1400 for the wider romance; the short recension's separate date is not independently established

assertion.notes: The broad normalized bounds represent the verbal catalog range; they are not a precise palaeographic dating.

dateStatements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century, in Cambridge, Magdalene College, Pepys MS 2014

assertion.notes: The first installment explicitly says zum erstenmal herausgegeben; the edition was completed in the next volume.

dateStatements.display: 1903-1904, first published by Rudolf Fischer in two Archiv installments

extent.description: The XML has two DIV1 elements: one poem division with 27 editorial verse groups and 3,114 L elements, and one commentary division with two prose paragraphs. Thirty-one PB elements represent printed pp. 289-298 and 25-45. Fischer says the partial version lacks the first 812 verses; the current Database of Middle English Romance says it lacks 814. Those source-lineation claims are preserved as an unresolved discrepancy rather than subtracted from the XML count.

formStatements.description: The cataloged medieval work is a rhyming-couplet poem; two brief German prose paragraphs are retained editorial framing rather than a substantive prose component of the medieval work.
Octosyllabic rhyming couplets, with substantial metrical irregularity noted by later editors.
Two German prose paragraphs by Fischer.

notes: The preferred title follows current scholarship but adds a witness/version qualifier so the viewer does not present Fischer's historically misleading title as an identity claim.
The catalog summary retains German because CME00072 includes Fischer's German commentary; German is not a language of the medieval poem.
The anti-Judaism and antisemitism terms provide necessary content warning and research discovery. They describe the poem's rhetoric and violence, not Jewish history.

Fischer's first installment and the modern witness catalog disagree with his 'thirteenth-century' subtitle: current scholarship dates Pepys 2014 to the end of the fourteenth or start of the fifteenth century and the wider work to 1375-1400.

next_steps: Compare the short-version Pepys text against the current long-version lineation and Phyllis Moe's version study; identify whether the difference reflects editions, couplet counting, or a corrected alignment.
openQuestions.question: Why does Fischer say the Pepys partial version lacks the first 812 verses while the current Database of Middle English Romance says it lacks 814 lines?

next_steps: Review the full version study and stemmatic scholarship; do not transfer either broad-work composition or witness-copying place to the recension without explicit evidence.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the short recension formed, as distinct from the broader romance's 1375-1400 London composition and the Northamptonshire copying of Pepys 2014?

next_steps: Consult McKitterick and Beadle, Catalogue of the Pepys Library, V.i, or ask the Pepys Librarian for the exact entry and any corrections; the College's current guide says there is no online Pepys catalog.
openQuestions.question: What additional codicological, scribal, and provenance facts does the current printed Pepys medieval-manuscript catalog provide for MS 2014?

assertion.notes: The specialist database localizes the wider romance to London; it does not separately localize the formation of the short recension.
This is the manuscript's reported place of origin and must not be transferred to composition.

scope.description: Post-transcription collation against Pepys MS 2014 for Fischer's edition

subjects.label: Christian anti-Judaism, antisemitism, and anti-Jewish violence
notes: This discovery heading records and contextualizes harmful polemical content. It does not endorse the poem's collective blame, supersessionism, dehumanization, or violence against Jews.

subjects.label: Divine vengeance, punishment, and Christian supersession
notes: The subject statement describes the poem's ideology rather than accepting its theology or historical claims.

subjects.label: Conversion, baptism, relics, and miraculous healing

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English romance recasts the Roman destruction of Jerusalem as Christian divine vengeance for Christ's death. The surviving Pepys short-version text begins after its lost or omitted opening, recounts portents and the martyrdom of James, the illnesses and conversions of Vespasian and Titus, Veronica's image-bearing cloth, the Roman siege, famine and cannibalism, Josephus's escape, and Titus's healing, then breaks off before the wider romance's conclusion. The poem repeatedly blames Jews collectively and uses graphic anti-Jewish violence, torture, dehumanization, and supersessionist theology; those features are documented here for responsible discovery and study, not repeated as historical truth or endorsed. The encoded unit concludes with two German prose paragraphs in which Fischer comments on the partial version and its relation to the wider Vengeaunce tradition.

CME00072 encodes Fischer's poem text from printed pp. 289-298 in Archiv 111 and pp. 25-45 in Archiv 112, followed by the two German paragraphs at the bottom of p. 45. It omits Fischer's edition introduction on pp. 285-288. The first installment ends with a continuation notice; the second calls itself the conclusion, although the Pepys witness and poem are textually imperfect.

The current title and short-version classification come from later scholarship. Fischer's source title Vindicta Salvatoris is retained as an alternate historical edition title. The work is related to but not identical with Latin and Old English Vindicta Salvatoris texts, and it must not be confused with the separate alliterative Siege of Jerusalem.

notes: The base Pepys copy has no verified medieval title at this locus. The parenthetical qualifier is modern discovery language, not a manuscript rubric.
titles.supplied_by: General Corpus, following the current Middle English Texts Series title while distinguishing the short recension and its base witness
titles.value: The Destruction of Jerusalem (short version, Pepys 2014)

notes: The editors explicitly describe this as an editorial title for the wider work tradition; their own edition uses the long version.
titles.supplied_by: Kara L. McShane and Mark J. B. Wright

notes: This historical editorial title should not collapse the poem into the distinct Latin or Old English Vindicta Salvatoris traditions. Fischer's own closing note reclassified the text as a partial version of the Vengeaunce/Battle tradition.
Fischer presents this as a name for the wider poem, not as a verified Pepys rubric.
This name is not evidence that the work is the separate alliterative Siege of Jerusalem.
Fischer rejects the heading as a work identification; it is retained as reception and attribution history, not a preferred title.

scope.description: Later attribution heading over the manuscript text

titles.supplied_by: Two later seventeenth-century hands above the Pepys text, as reported by Fischer

work.summary: An anonymous Middle English romance recasts the Roman destruction of Jerusalem as Christian divine vengeance for Christ's death. The surviving Pepys short-version text begins after its lost or omitted opening, recounts portents and the martyrdom of James, the illnesses and conversions of Vespasian and Titus, Veronica's image-bearing cloth, the Roman siege, famine and cannibalism, Josephus's escape, and Titus's healing, then breaks off before the wider romance's conclusion. The poem repeatedly blames Jews collectively and uses graphic anti-Jewish violence, torture, dehumanization, and supersessionist theology; those features are documented here for responsible discovery and study, not repeated as historical truth or endorsed. The encoded unit concludes with two German prose paragraphs in which Fischer comments on the partial version and its relation to the wider Vengeaunce tradition.
